response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend my time out there scrolling, mostly when I’ve finished a long day on the job or when I’m taking a break from cycling. And honestly, the garbage out there—all that fake stuff—it just gets under your skin. It’s like trying to build something solid when the foundation is made of sand. With all the noise, it’s easy to get worked up, and with my tendency to feel things strongly, the anger can build up fast. But you gotta learn to keep your head straight, you know? It’s about being a good builder of your own reality online. The first thing I do, and this is important because I’m pretty outgoing, but I also get stressed out easily, is to slow down. Don't just react to the headline; stop, take a breath, and look at it like you’re inspecting a piece of lumber. Is the grain right? Does it look strong? Before you share or even get mad in the comments, you have to check the source. Who is saying this? Are they some random account that popped up last week, or is it someone with a history? It takes effort, but it’s better than letting that anxiety spiral. I try to treat social media like a busy street in the city—you see a lot of things, but you don't stop and argue with every hot dog cart vendor. You observe, you filter, and you move on. If something feels completely off, if it’s designed just to make you furious, I just mute it or unfollow it. It’s not about being weak; it’s about protecting the peace, which, as a Christian, I know is important. My creativity tells me there are better things to focus on—like getting out on the bike or getting lost in a good novel—instead of wrestling with digital lies.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an extroverted, highly neurotic 29-year-old self-employed carpenter living in NYC, who spends a few times a week on social media. The tone must reflect her high energy, tendency toward anxiety/stress, but also her creative nature and strong moral grounding (Christianity). The advice needs to be practical and grounded, relating to building/construction metaphors (due to her trade) and her emotional state. The response emphasizes slowing down, critical source checking (like inspecting wood), filtering out negativity to maintain peace, and redirecting energy to constructive hobbies.
response_parsed.tone: Energetic, candid, slightly anxious but resolute, grounded in practical experience.
citations: Self-reflection and common media literacy practices
